## Founders section

**Q-FOUND-2 — Have any of you written code, or done other technical work on your product?**
Yes. Nathan (CTO) has written all production code: payment integration (PayPal), listing flow, search, booking system, host/guest profiles. No non-founder has written any production code. Joe and Brian designed the UI/UX (industrial design backgrounds — they sketch and prototype, then Nathan builds). No outsourced engineering.

**Q-FOUND-3 — Are you looking for a co-founder?**
No. Brian, Joe, and Nathan are the founding team. Brian and Joe are college roommates (4 years pre-founding); Nathan joined early 2008 from his Datamine exit, via mutual networks (we'd known of his Datamine work for 2 years before approaching him).

[Per-founder profiles complete and gating-validated. See factual-fills.md for details.]

## Company section

**Q-CO-2 — Describe what your company does in 50 characters or less.**
Book rooms with locals, not hotels.

**Q-CO-7 — What is your company going to make?**
We've built airbedandbreakfast.com — an online marketplace where travelers book a spot in a stranger's home (couch, air mattress, spare room, or whole apartment) for a night or longer, instead of a hotel. Hosts list their space with photos, set price, define availability. Travelers search by destination + date + price, view reviews, book via PayPal. We hold payment in escrow until check-in. Service fee: 3% guest, 3% host. Currently US-only; ~10,000 listings.

**Q-CO-8/9 — Where do you live now / where would the company be based after YC?**
Currently: SF / SF / SF. After YC: all three founders stay in SF. We chose SF before YC because hosts and travelers in SF are early adopters; the network effects of a marketplace require dense early geography. Post-YC we expand inventory beyond SF but founder location stays SF — partners, technical talent, and the customer-discovery loop all live here.

---

## Progress section

**Q-PROG-1 — How far along are you?**
Production marketplace live for 14 months. ~10,000 listings, ~3,000 active hosts (booking in last 60 days). $13.4k April 2009 net revenue, ~33% mo/mo growth. Major launches: SF (origin Oct 2007), DNC Denver (Aug 2008 — proved event-overflow model). Cereal-box bridge financing kept us alive through investor "no" period (Oct-Nov 2008, $30k revenue from limited-edition Obama O's / Cap'n McCain's). Three founders full-time since early 2008; living frugally; ~4 months runway.

**Q-PROG-2 — How long have each of you been working on this? Full-time?**
Brian and Joe: 19 months (since October 2007), full-time since January 2008. Nathan: 16 months, full-time since joining in early 2008. All three 100% Airbnb since respective full-time start dates — no side projects, no part-time consulting, no other jobs.

**Q-PROG-3 — Tech stack**
Rails on AWS EC2. PostgreSQL. PayPal payment processing. S3 for listing photos. Memcached for search caching. AI/ML: none currently — search ranking is rule-based. Roadmap: image-quality scoring for listings. Front-end: jQuery + custom CSS. Mobile: not yet. Private GitHub repo.

**Q-PROG-4 — Coding agent session [Summer 2026+ NEW]**
N/A for 2009. (For 2026+ applicants: skill produces dedicated handler per Q-PROG-4 atom pointers.)

**Q-PROG-5/6 — Are people using your product?**
Yes. ~10,000 listings, thousands of bookings since launch. We are NOT yet at PMF — hosts list aggressively but only 30% of listings receive a booking in any given month, repeat-guest rate is 18%. Active: 10,000 hosts listed, 3,000 active. Both sides pay (3% + 3% fees). Largest single booking: $4,200 (NYC Fashion Week 14-night). Largest concentrated host: $8,400 in 8 days during DNC.

Specific user love-evidence: Marie L., 29-year-old engineer from London, flying in for a startup conference: "I'd been trying to find a place for two weeks, every hotel was booked, and your platform was the only thing that worked." Returned 6 weeks later.

We haven't yet figured out how to make hosts list during off-peak. Event-overflow demand is real and loud; steady-state demand is quieter and unproven.

**Q-PROG-7/8 — Revenue (6-month USD breakdown)**

| Month | $USD |
|---|---|
| Nov 2008 | 4,200 |
| Dec 2008 | 5,600 |
| Jan 2009 | 7,300 |
| Feb 2009 | 8,800 |
| Mar 2009 | 11,200 |
| Apr 2009 | 13,400 |

~33% month-over-month average. Cumulative ~$50,500 over 6 months. Cereal-box revenue ($30k Oct 2008) was non-recurring bridge — excluded from this trajectory.

We're at $13.4k April 2009 — well below Series A traction calibration ($150-250k MRR per Seibel) but above PG default-alive growth threshold (>5x annually). Right stage for YC, not for Series A.

**Q-PROG-9 — Revenue source breakdown**
100% from marketplace take rate (3% guest + 3% host on each booking). No subscription, no advertising, no consulting.

**Q-PROG-11 — Re-applicant**
N/A — first-time application.

**Q-PROG-12 — Other accelerators**
None.

## Idea section

**Q-IDEA-1 — Why this idea / domain expertise / how do you know people need it**

October 2007, San Francisco. Joe and I were broke RISD grads. The IDSA conference came to town and every hotel sold out — designers we wanted to meet were planning to sleep in their cars. We rented air mattresses on our living-room floor for $80/night. Three guests stayed four days. They came back the next year.

Travelers don't want hotels. They want a place to sleep at the right price in the right location. When hotels run out (conferences, festivals, political conventions), travelers will accept a stranger's air mattress IF they trust the listing.

The trust unlock is photos + reviews + payment escrow, all three at once. Couchsurfing has trust without payment, so hosts can't professionalize. Craigslist has payment without trust, so listings stay spam. The gap is profitable.

We've run airbedandbreakfast.com since 2008. We rebuilt the listing flow three times. We launched at the DNC in Denver — sold-out hotels, demand surge, model proven. 10,000+ listings now. Nathan joined as CTO from his Datamine exit.

We know people need this because we watch them use it. Most enthusiastic user: a London engineer flying in for a startup conference: "Every hotel was booked, and your platform was the only thing that worked."

**Q-IDEA-2 — Who are your competitors? What do you understand about your business that they don't?**

**Couchsurfing.** Hosts and trust, no payment. Philosophy rejects monetization — organizational conflict. Hosts can't professionalize without paying-customer feedback.

**Craigslist (vacation rentals).** Payment, no trust. No photos required, no reviews, no escrow. Philosophy rejects verticalization. Listings stay spam.

**VRBO / HomeAway.** Whole-home leisure rentals, multi-day. Different inventory and use case from our nights/work-travel.

**Hotels (Expedia, Hotwire, Hilton).** Inventory and trust, cannot broker peer inventory. Listing private homes cannibalizes hotel pricing — business-model conflict. Teams built around hotel-supplier relationships, not consumer-host onboarding — organizational conflict.

**What we understand others don't:** Travelers don't want hotels — they want a place to sleep at the right price in the right location. The trust unlock for stranger's couch substituting for a hotel is photos + reviews + payment escrow at once. We're the only entity with all three plus peer inventory. Each competitor has a structural reason they can't follow.

**Q-IDEA-3 — How will you make money? How much could you make?**
Service fee on each booking: ~3% guest + ~3% host. Take rate ~6%. Beachhead is event overflow (proven). Expansion: US-tier-1 business travel routes (12 months) → international leisure (24-36 months) → whole-home rentals (36+ months). US lodging market ~$120B/yr. Capturing 1% at 6% take rate = $72M/yr. $100M+ annual revenue achievable in 5-7 years if we stay disciplined.

**Q-IDEA-4 — Category**
Marketplace / Consumer.

**Q-IDEA-5 — Other ideas considered**

1. **Snooth** — wine-discovery platform. Considered: friend in wine industry had specific frustrations. Didn't pursue: not wine-experts, regulatory navigation (state-by-state), tiny addressable customer (sommeliers).

2. **Conference-scoped travel marketplace** — early version of Airbnb but ONLY for conferences. Discovered first (IDSA experiment). Didn't stick: same trust infrastructure could serve broader travel; conferences ~3% of US travel volume.

3. **Peer-to-peer goods rental marketplace.** Same trust-infrastructure idea on neighbor's drill / camera / lawn mower. Didn't pursue: lower repeat purchase, harder trust unlock (theft vs. lodging where host is present), the 6-month repeat-host cohort suggested marketplace was right model but lodging was right inventory type.
